text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

The world's best surfers will compete for gold on the largest island in French Polynesia despite concerns about the environmental impact

PARIS — There are no consistent surf spots close to Paris. The nearest are in and around Biarritz and Hossegor, just north of the Basque country, close to the Spanish border – around 500 miles away from the French capital.

But they are a darn sight closer than Tahiti, where theParis 2024surfing competition is being held.

The choice of Teahupo’o as the venue forthe Olympic Gamesappeared at first glance the epitome of French haughtiness – we have the world’s best spot in one of our colonies and,Mon Dieu, we are going to exploit it.

It was a decision that was met with enthusiasm by the athletes, especially those who are not of the calibre of those inthe World Surf League, the top-level competition circuit. But it was far less popular among locals.

Resistance to the Olympic circus coming to town for the weekend grew when the International Olympic Committee (IOC) announced that it needed to build a more permanent judging tower than the temporary wooden one that had been built and dismantled yearly since the early 2000s.

The IOC said the tower needed to be made of aluminium and required air-conditioning and toilets. And that it needed to be built on the fragile coral reef just out of reach of the wave.

Resistance came from the biggest name in the sport, Kelly Slater, who said last year: “It doesn’t make sense to build such a huge tower for a two-day event.”

And the residents of the village on the shore had legitimate fears that their way of life would be irrevocably changed.

It is far from a surf resort – there is not even a surf shop there, let alone an apres-surf bar. Newquay it is not.

Peva Levy, a Teahupo’o resident one of the first to surf the spot, said: “It was too much for us, a big change. And it was just for, like, one week.”

Teahupo’o (pronounced te-ah-hu-poh’oh and loosely translated to “place of skulls”) is an awe-inspiring location, to give Paris 2024 organisers their due.

And although it is roughly 10,000 miles from Paris, the impact of flying 48 surfers there is not as great as if they had flown to France, as the majority of competitors are from Pacific Rim and South American countries. Two of France’s four representatives are from French Polynesia. One lives in Reunion – hardly a short hop from Paris.

The wave itself is mesmerising, if also deadly – it appears to bend laws of physics on big swells.

A huge wall of blue liquid rises out of the deep, the lip feathers in the offshore wind, before the wall bends, curls and explodes on the shallow water covering a reef, creating a tube wider than it is high. The sea level drops by at least two feet in the vortex of the chaos.

Australian world No 5 Ethan Ewing, who broke his back in a wipeout at Teahupo’o in August 2023, said: “The energy of this wave is like nothing else.

“It’s just so raw and has so much power.

“Even now there are still nerves. It’s just one of those waves, it’s got big consequences.”

But a few days of beautiful images was going to come at a massive cost to the local environment and the people who live there every day.

So the IOC scaled back its plans. Where it was going to build an athletes’ village for the 48 surfers and their respective medical and support staff, it now houses them on a cruise ship berthed offshore.

And the tower is now collapsible, like the previous one. It has been placed in the same spot as the old one – where there is minimal coral growth, according to surf photographer Tim McKenna, who visited Teahupo’o just before the final test event before the Olympics. And it doesn’t appear to have air conditioning.

The head of the International Surfing Association, Fernando Aguerre of Argentina, faced questions this week on why the Olympic surfing competition is in Tahiti and not the south-west of France.

He said: “It’s very simple. In south-western France, the surfing area of France, some people call it the California of France, in July and August, there are barely any waves.

“You could be two weeks looking at flat ocean. We knew that.

“We are so lucky because in South Pacific France, July and August are the best months of the year [for waves].

“These waves [in Teahupo’o] originate in winds that blow super hard from low pressures that spin off Antarctica.

“They are the same swells that make amazing waves this time of the year in Bali or in South Africa or in Australia or the west coast of the Americas.”

And did the IOC’s changes of plan reduce the resistance from locals?

Cindy Otcenasek, the head of one of the largest protest groups, whose petition garnered over 200,000 signatures, said before the final contest before the Games that they had “thrown in the towel”, adding that they had done “everything that was possible within the limits of the law”.

Polynesians have a strong connection to their surroundings, holding the belief that land, water and weather are governed by their respective spirits.

Some have said that if Teahupo’o doesn’t want the Olympics descending upon it, the waves will not appear throughout the Games and unseasonable onshore winds will blow.

And if Teahupo’o doesn’t want a shiny aluminium judging tower blotting the seascape, no matter how temporary, a massive swell will mow it down.

Aguerre toed the party line when he was asked this week about the plight of the 1,500 or so people who call Teahupo’o home.

He said: “It’s a local community that will continue to be what it is, a small town with fishing, living off nature.

“It’s not a tourist spot, it’s the end of the road, literally. This is what we all want, the local people, the visitors, the IOC, the surfers. It’s very respectful.

“What is being done for the contest is going to be taken down and taken away.

“I don’t think any of those fears [that the reef would be affected by the construction of a judges’ tower] were substantiated or were confirmed by reality.

“The tower is where the old tower used to be and after the end of the competition, the tower comes out and it goes to land.

“It’s a happy ending.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: Why Olympic surfing is taking place 10,000 miles from Paris
author: Matt Butler
description: The world's best surfers will compete for gold on the largest island in French Polynesia despite concerns about the environmental impact
